claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to start by asking you about your child.

**First, let me ask: How old is your child?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** automatically qualify (subject to other conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **20 or over** generally don't qualify for Child Benefit

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, let me know about all of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Yes, I'd like to check about Child Benefit. I have three children actually - Alex is 5, Blake is 17, and Charlie is 19. They all live with me here at home. Can you help me work out if I'm eligible?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can definitely help you check eligibility for all three of your children - Alex (5), Blake (17), and Charlie (19).

Let me work through each child's situation:

**For Alex (age 5):** Since Alex is under 16, they automatically qualify as a qualifying child, but I need to check one important thing first.

**Is Alex working full-time (meaning 24 hours or more per week)?**

(I'm quite sure the answer is no for a 5-year-old, but I need to confirm this as part of the official process!)

After we confirm this, I'll then ask about Blake (17) and Charlie (19), as they're in the 16-19 age category where education and training status matters.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - Alex is only 5, so definitely not working at all! He's just at primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's what I expected. So **Alex qualifies as a qualifying child** since he's under 16 and not working full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now let me check on **Blake (age 17)** and **Charlie (age 19)**. Since they're both in the 16-19 age range, their eligibility depends on their education or training status.

**Let me ask about Blake first:**

**1. Is Blake currently in approved education or training?** This includes:
   - Full-time non-advanced education (like A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQ Level 3 or below)
   - Approved training programs
   - Home education that's been approved

**2. Is Blake working 24 hours or more per week?**

Please tell me about Blake's current situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake isn't in any education or training at the moment - he left sixth form earlier this year and hasn't gone back. And no, he's not working either, just at home figuring out what to do next.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Blake. Since Blake is 17, left sixth form earlier this year, and is not currently in approved education or training, **Blake would not qualify as a qualifying child** for Child Benefit purposes. The rules require that children aged 16-19 must be in approved education or training to qualify.

Now let me ask about **Charlie (age 19)**:

**Is Charlie currently in approved education or training?** This would include:
- Full-time non-advanced education (like A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQ Level 3 or below)
- Approved training programs

And is Charlie working 24 hours or more per week?

Please tell me about Charlie's current situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie is still in education - he's doing his A-levels at the local sixth form college. He's not working, just focused on his studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's helpful information. Since Charlie is 19 and doing A-levels (which is full-time non-advanced education), he may still qualify. Let me check a couple more details about Charlie's situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - A-levels are approved education. Now I need to check a few more important details about Charlie:

**1. When did Charlie turn 19?** (This is important because Child Benefit stops on the child's 20th birthday, even if they're still in education)

**2. Is Charlie's education course at least 12 hours per week of supervised study?** (For A-levels at sixth form college, this is typically yes, but I need to confirm)

**3. Is Charlie registered with any benefits in their own name** (like Universal Credit, Income Support, or tax credits)?

Please let me know these details about Charlie.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie turned 19 back in March this year, so he's got a while until he's 20. Yes, his A-level course is definitely more than 12 hours a week - it's full-time at the college. And no, he's not claiming any benefits himself, he just lives at home with us.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's very helpful. So Charlie turned 19 in March (so won't turn 20 for several months), is doing full-time A-levels (definitely more than 12 hours per week), and isn't claiming any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me navigate properly through the assessment. Since we're checking Charlie who is in education and not working full-time:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Charlie's A-levels are approved non-advanced education. Now let me continue checking:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You mentioned Charlie is not working, so he's working less than 24 hours per week:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You confirmed Charlie is not claiming any benefits, so:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Charlie doing an apprenticeship, or is he just doing regular A-levels at sixth form college?**

(I need to confirm this, as apprenticeships have different rules)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's just doing regular A-levels at sixth form college, not an apprenticeship or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! So Charlie is doing regular A-levels (not an apprenticeship), which means **Charlie qualifies as a qualifying child** for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check a few things about **your** situation as the claimant. 

**Do you normally live in the UK?** You mentioned earlier that all three children live with you at home - is your home in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, we all live here in the UK together - I live in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! So you normally live in the UK (in Manchester), which meets the residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You mentioned that all three children live with you at home, so you're clearly responsible for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Now I need to check: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie?** (For example, another parent or guardian?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them. I'm the only one looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! So there's no other claimant for the children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check the children's living situation. You mentioned they all live with you at home. 

**Are any of the children:**
- Currently in hospital for an extended stay?
- In local authority care?
- In a fostering arrangement?

Or are they all just living normally at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're all just living normally at home with me - none of them are in hospital, care, or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! So all three children are living normally at home with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
One final question: **Is the local council paying towards any of the children's accommodation or maintenance costs?**

(For example, are you receiving any payments from the local authority specifically to help with the costs of looking after the children?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards looking after them - they're just my kids living at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Thank you for all that information. I now have a complete picture of your situation. Let me provide you with the eligibility assessment for all three of your children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results**

Based on the information you've provided, here's the outcome for each of your children:

---

### ✅ **ALEX (age 5) - ELIGIBLE**
You **can claim Child Benefit** for Alex. As a child under 16 who is not working, Alex automatically qualifies as a qualifying child.

---

### ❌ **BLAKE (age 17) - NOT ELIGIBLE**
Unfortunately, you **cannot claim Child Benefit** for Blake at this time. Although Blake is in the 16-19 age range, the eligibility rules require that young people in this age group must be in approved education or training to qualify. Since Blake left sixth form and is not currently in any education, training, or approved apprenticeship program, Blake does not meet the qualifying child criteria.

**Important note for Blake:** If Blake decides to return to education (like college, sixth form, or approved training), or enrolls in certain approved programs, you may be able to claim Child Benefit for Blake again at that point. You would need to notify HMRC of the change in circumstances.

---

### ✅ **CHARLIE (age 19) - ELIGIBLE**
You **can claim Child Benefit** for Charlie. Even though Charlie is 19, he qualifies because:
- He's doing A-levels (approved non-advanced education)
- His course is full-time (more than 12 hours per week)
- He's not working 24+ hours per week
- He's not claiming benefits in his own right
- He won't turn 20 until next March (benefit continues until 20th birthday)

---

## **Next Steps to Claim:**

Since you're eligible to claim for **Alex and Charlie**, you should:

1. **Apply for Child Benefit** - You can apply online or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and each child
3. **Provide birth certificates** (or adoption certificates) for Alex and Charlie
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the benefit through your tax return (though you're still eligible to claim)
5. **Payments** are typically made every 4 weeks

You can find the application and more information here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

---

Do you have any questions about this assessment or the application process?

reasonings: Alex is 5 years old, which is under 16. Children under 16 automatically qualify as qualifying children for Child Benefit, provided they are not working 24+ hours per week. Alex is only at primary school and not working at all. Alex lives with the claimant in the UK, the claimant is responsible for Alex, no one else is claiming for Alex, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Therefore, Alex qualifies for Child Benefit.

Blake is 17 years old, which falls in the 16-19 age category. For children aged 16-19 to qualify for Child Benefit, they must be in approved education or training. Blake left sixth form earlier this year and is not currently in any education or training program. Blake is also not working. Because Blake does not meet the requirement of being in approved education or training, Blake does not qualify as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes.

Charlie is 19 years old (turned 19 in March), which falls in the 16-19 age category. Charlie is doing A-levels at sixth form college, which is approved non-advanced education. The course is full-time (more than the required 12 hours per week of supervised study). Charlie is not working (less than 24 hours per week), is not receiving any benefits in his own right, and is not doing an apprenticeship. Charlie won't turn 20 until March next year, so is still within the age limit. Charlie lives with the claimant in the UK, the claimant is responsible for Charlie, no one else is claiming for Charlie, and the local council is not paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance. Therefore, Charlie qualifies for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in Manchester with three children: Alex (5), Blake (17), and Charlie (19). The claimant meets UK residency requirements, is responsible for all three children (they all live together), and no one else is claiming for any of them. The local council is not paying towards any of the children's costs. Alex qualifies as he is under 16 and not working. Blake does not qualify as he is 16-19 but not in approved education or training. Charlie qualifies as he is 19, in approved education (A-levels), not working full-time, not receiving benefits, and won't turn 20 until March next year. The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie, but not for Blake.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)
